_purpose: Local economy timeline for the Lower Cape Fear / Wilmington regional cluster (New Hanover, Brunswick, Pender, Columbus, Bladen counties). Covers 1725-1960 across seven economic eras — from colonial rice culture and naval stores through WWII shipbuilding and military installations. The Cape Fear was NC's only deep-water port, and every economic era in this cluster was shaped by that fact: the river brought goods in, carried commodities out, and made Wilmington the state's largest city until the railroad age redistributed growth inland.
display_name: Lower Cape Fear / Wilmington — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.era_name: Colonial Rice Culture and Naval Stores
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Tidal rice cultivation on the lower Cape Fear, naval stores (tar, pitch, turpentine) from longleaf pine forests, and Wilmington port commerce
economic_eras.description: The Lower Cape Fear was settled later than most of coastal NC — the first permanent settlers arrived in the 1720s, many from the established rice-growing colony of South Carolina. They brought rice culture, enslaved Africans with rice-growing expertise, and a plantation model that distinguished the Lower Cape Fear from the yeoman-farmer interior. Tidal rice fields along the Brunswick County shore and the lower Cape Fear required massive capital investment in dikes, canals, and trunk gates, and depended entirely on enslaved labor — particularly enslaved Africans from the Rice Coast of West Africa (modern Senegal, Gambia, Sierra Leone) who possessed generations of rice-growing knowledge. Naval stores production in the longleaf pine forests of Bladen, Columbus, and what would become Pender County provided a secondary export commodity. Wilmington (founded as New Liverpool/Newton, incorporated 1739) grew rapidly as the colony's only deep-water port, handling rice, naval stores, lumber, and deerskins for export to Britain and the West Indies. Brunswick Town, at the mouth of the Cape Fear, served as the original port before Wilmington's superior river position won out.

county_specifics.note: Wilmington was the colony's premier port and commercial center. The town's merchants, factors, and lawyers formed an urban elite with direct ties to London and Charleston. Enslaved people constituted a majority of the town's population by 1770.

Tidal rice plantations along the lower Cape Fear and its tributaries made Brunswick the wealthiest agricultural area in NC. The Moores, Howes, and other planter families built rice estates rivaling those of the South Carolina lowcountry. Brunswick Town served as the county seat and colonial port before Wilmington's rise.

Enormous when formed in 1734, covering most of what is now southeastern NC. Naval stores dominated the economy on the pine uplands. The Cape Fear River was the commercial lifeline, carrying barrels of tar and turpentine to Wilmington. Settlement was thin and dispersed.

Not yet a separate county (created 1808 from Bladen and Brunswick). The area was longleaf pine forest with scattered turpentine operations and subsistence farms. The Waccamaw River provided water access to the coast.
Not yet a separate county (created 1875 from New Hanover). The area along the Northeast Cape Fear River was naval stores country with turpentine camps and small farms.

key_infrastructure.name: Port of Wilmington
key_infrastructure.impact: NC's only deep-water port. The Cape Fear River was navigable for ocean-going vessels to Wilmington, 30 miles upriver from the Atlantic. This single geographic fact made the Lower Cape Fear the colony's primary commercial corridor.

key_infrastructure.name: Brunswick Town and Fort Johnston
key_infrastructure.impact: Brunswick Town was the original settlement and port at the mouth of the Cape Fear. Fort Johnston (1749) guarded the river entrance. Brunswick Town declined as Wilmington grew, but the military presence at the river mouth would remain strategically important through the Civil War.

key_infrastructure.name: Tidal rice field infrastructure (Brunswick County)
key_infrastructure.impact: Elaborate systems of dikes, canals, and trunk gates transformed tidal marshes into productive rice fields. This infrastructure represented enormous capital investment and enslaved engineering labor. Once abandoned, these fields reverted to marsh.

key_infrastructure.name: Crown bounty on naval stores
key_infrastructure.impact: Parliamentary bounties subsidized NC naval stores production. The Lower Cape Fear's longleaf pine forests and river access to Wilmington's port made the region a leading supplier. Loss of the bounty after independence disrupted the export market.

major_employers.name: Rice plantations (enslaved labor)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of enslaved workers on the largest plantations. Orton Plantation and neighboring estates employed the largest concentrations of enslaved labor in colonial NC.

major_employers.name: Wilmington port operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Stevedores, sailors, coopers, shipwrights, pilots, and merchants. Port employment was the primary non-agricultural livelihood in the region.

major_employers.name: Naval stores operators
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of small operations across the pine belt, each employing enslaved laborers or small free crews

wage_benchmarks.occupation: Hired-out enslaved laborer (rice field or naval stores)
wage_benchmarks.source: Carney, Judith A. Black Rice: The African Origins of Rice Cultivation in the Americas. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2001, ch. 4.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines, ch. 2.
Watson, Alan D. Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861, ch. 3.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in the Lower Cape Fear in the {decade}s, the river was the economy — rice moved downstream from the Brunswick County plantations, tar and turpentine floated down from the pine forests of Bladen, and everything passed through the wharves at Wilmington on its way to London or the West Indies.
economic_eras.source: Lee, Lawrence. The Lower Cape Fear in Colonial Days. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1965. Carney, Judith A. Black Rice: The African Origins of Rice Cultivation in the Americas. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2001. Watson, Alan D. Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2003.

economic_eras.era_name: Antebellum Port Economy and Turpentine Boom
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Wilmington port commerce, naval stores (turpentine) as leading export commodity, rice declining, railroad construction transforming the region's economic geography
economic_eras.description: The Lower Cape Fear economy between 1790 and 1860 revolved around three forces: Wilmington's dominance as NC's premier port, the turpentine boom that made North Carolina the world's leading producer of naval stores, and the Wilmington & Weldon Railroad (1840) that transformed the city from a river port into a railroad hub. Rice cultivation declined steadily after the Revolution as the Lower Cape Fear plantations could not compete with the massive operations of the Georgia and South Carolina lowcountry. Turpentine replaced rice as the region's most valuable export — the longleaf pine forests of Bladen, Columbus, and the Pender area produced crude gum that was distilled into spirits of turpentine and rosin, shipped downriver to Wilmington, and exported worldwide. Wilmington grew to become NC's largest city by 1860 (population approximately 9,500), with a commercial district, banks, newspapers, and an urban culture unique in the state. The enslaved population constituted approximately half of the city and a majority in the rural counties.

county_specifics.note: Wilmington was NC's largest city and its commercial, financial, and cultural center. The Wilmington & Weldon Railroad (1840) and the Wilmington & Manchester Railroad (1854) made it a railroad hub as well as a port. The city had a significant free Black population — artisans, stevedores, draymen — alongside the enslaved majority.

Rice cultivation continued in decline. Smithville (later Southport) at the river mouth served as a pilot station and fishing town. Fort Caswell was built in the 1820s-30s to guard the river entrance. The county's plantation economy was fading.

Turpentine production was the primary economy. Elizabethtown on the Cape Fear bluffs served as the county seat and a modest river port. Mixed farming (corn, sweet potatoes, livestock) sustained non-turpentine families.

Formed in 1808 from Bladen and Brunswick. Turpentine and lumber dominated. Whiteville, the county seat, was a small courthouse village. The Waccamaw Siouan community around Lake Waccamaw existed largely outside the county's commercial economy.

Not yet a separate county (created 1875). The Northeast Cape Fear River corridor was turpentine country. Holly Shelter Swamp separated the eastern section from the interior. This was one of the most intensive turpentine districts in NC.

key_infrastructure.name: Wilmington & Weldon Railroad
key_infrastructure.impact: At 161 miles, the longest railroad in the world at completion. Connected Wilmington to the Virginia border, channeling the commerce of eastern NC through Wilmington's port. Made the city the railroad hub of the state and attracted new warehousing and manufacturing.

key_infrastructure.name: Wilmington & Manchester Railroad
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Wilmington to South Carolina, extending the city's commercial reach south. Ran through Brunswick County, providing the county its first railroad access.

key_infrastructure.name: Turpentine distilleries (Wilmington)
key_infrastructure.impact: Wilmington became the world's largest turpentine market by the 1840s. Distilleries along the waterfront processed crude gum into spirits of turpentine and rosin for export. The industry employed hundreds of free and enslaved workers.

key_infrastructure.name: Cape Fear Navigation Company improvements
key_infrastructure.impact: State-chartered company cleared obstructions and improved navigation on the Cape Fear River, maintaining the river as a commercial artery for the interior counties alongside the new railroads.

major_employers.name: Wilmington port and railroad operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands — stevedores, draymen, railroad workers, coopers, shipwrights, warehouse workers, merchants, and factors. The largest concentration of non-agricultural employment in NC.

major_employers.name: Turpentine operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of enslaved workers across the pine belt. Turpentine was the most physically demanding work in the antebellum South outside of sugar cane.

major_employers.name: Rice plantations (declining)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining from colonial peak. Orton and neighboring plantations maintained reduced operations.

wage_benchmarks.source: Watson, Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861, ch. 5.
Outland, Tapping the Pines, ch. 4.
Watson, Wilmington, to 1861, ch. 5.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South, ch. 1.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, Wilmington was the biggest city in North Carolina and the Lower Cape Fear was the wealthiest corner of the state — not because of cotton or tobacco, but because of turpentine. Every barrel that floated down the Cape Fear from {county} County passed through Wilmington's wharves, and the city's merchants grew rich on the trade while the men who bled the pines owned nothing.
economic_eras.source: Watson, Alan D. Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2003. Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 2004. Lee, Lawrence. The Lower Cape Fear in Colonial Days. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1965.

economic_eras.era_name: Civil War — Blockade Running and Fort Fisher
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Wartime economy — blockade running through Wilmington, military operations, collapse of civilian commerce, emancipation of enslaved labor force
economic_eras.description: The Civil War transformed the Lower Cape Fear into one of the most strategically important corridors in the Confederacy. Wilmington became the South's last open port after other Confederate harbors fell to Union forces. Blockade runners — fast, shallow-draft steamers loaded with cotton for export and returning with weapons, medicine, and supplies from Bermuda and Nassau — ran the Union naval blockade at the Cape Fear inlet, protected by Fort Fisher's massive earthwork batteries on the Brunswick County shore. The blockade-running trade generated enormous short-term wealth for Wilmington's merchants and ship operators, but the civilian economy suffered as normal commerce collapsed. The interior counties (Bladen, Columbus) experienced the war as deprivation — men conscripted, supplies requisitioned, turpentine operations disrupted. Fort Fisher fell to a massive Union amphibious assault in January 1865, and Wilmington was occupied by Federal forces on February 22, 1865. The emancipation of the region's enslaved majority — roughly half the population across the five-county area — eliminated the labor system that had sustained every sector of the economy.

county_specifics.note: Wilmington was the Confederacy's last open port. Blockade running generated immense profits for a few and economic disruption for many. The city was occupied by Union forces in February 1865. The Yellow Fever epidemic of 1862 killed hundreds.

Fort Fisher at the mouth of the Cape Fear was the largest earthwork fortification in the Confederacy. Its fall on January 15, 1865, after a combined naval bombardment and ground assault, was one of the war's final decisive battles. Smithville (Southport) was occupied by Union forces.
Isolated from direct military action but drained of men and resources. Conscription took working-age men, and impressment agents took livestock and supplies. The turpentine industry ground to a halt.

Similarly isolated. The county provided soldiers but saw no major engagements. Desertion and draft evasion were significant in the swampy interior, where fugitives could hide in the Waccamaw River bottoms.
Not yet a separate county. The area along the Northeast Cape Fear saw some military activity as Union forces pushed inland from the coast.

key_infrastructure.name: Fort Fisher (Brunswick County)
key_infrastructure.impact: Massive L-shaped earthwork fortification guarding the New Inlet of the Cape Fear River. Its survival kept Wilmington open to blockade runners until January 1865. Its fall sealed the Confederacy's fate — without Wilmington, the South had no remaining supply line.

key_infrastructure.name: Blockade running operations (Wilmington)
key_infrastructure.impact: Wilmington handled more blockade-running traffic than any other Confederate port. An estimated 397 successful runs were made. Profits were enormous — a single successful round trip could net $100,000 or more. The trade attracted British captains, Confederate agents, and war profiteers.

key_infrastructure.name: Wilmington & Weldon Railroad (wartime)
key_infrastructure.impact: The railroad carried supplies from Wilmington's blockade runners to the Confederate armies in Virginia — the 'lifeline of the Confederacy.' Its destruction by Union forces in 1865 was a military objective.

major_employers.name: Confederate military and Fort Fisher garrison
major_employers.employment_scale: Fort Fisher garrison of 1,500-2,000; total Confederate military presence in the region of several thousand.

major_employers.name: Blockade running operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of sailors, pilots, stevedores, and support workers. The Cape Fear river pilots — men who knew every shoal and channel — were critical.

major_employers.name: Wilmington salt works and wartime manufacturing
major_employers.employment_scale: Salt production along the coast employed hundreds, as imported salt was unavailable and salt was essential for meat preservation.

wage_benchmarks.source: Confederate War Department records.
Wise, Stephen R. Lifeline of the Confederacy, ch. 5.
Lonn, Ella. Salt as a Factor in the Confederacy, ch. 3.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: While {ancestor_name} was barely surviving in {county} County on what the war left behind, Wilmington's waterfront was piled with cotton bales and rifles — the blockade runners making fortunes measured in gold while the soldiers at Fort Fisher earned worthless Confederate paper.
economic_eras.source: Wise, Stephen R. Lifeline of the Confederacy: Blockade Running During the Civil War. Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1988. Fonvielle, Chris E., Jr. The Wilmington Campaign: Last Rays of Departing Hope. Campbell, CA: Savas Publishing, 1997. Gragg, Rod. Confederate Goliath: The Battle of Fort Fisher. New York: HarperCollins, 1991.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Lumber and timber extraction replacing naval stores; Wilmington port rebuilding; sharecropping and tenant farming; railroad reconstruction; biracial political economy under Reconstruction and Fusion rule
economic_eras.description: The postwar Lower Cape Fear economy rebuilt slowly around three pillars: Wilmington's port (diminished but still the state's largest), lumber extraction from the region's vast forests, and tenant farming on the reorganized plantations. The naval stores industry was in terminal decline as longleaf pine forests were exhausted, but the timber itself — longleaf pine and cypress — became the commodity. Sawmills appeared throughout Bladen, Columbus, and the Pender area, cutting lumber for export through Wilmington and for the railroad rebuilding that reconnected the region to national markets. Wilmington's Black community — the largest in NC — built institutions from scratch: churches, schools, mutual aid societies, and eventually political organizations. Under Reconstruction and the Fusion political alliance of the 1890s, Black men held elected office, served as police officers, and participated visibly in civic life. This era ended violently on November 10, 1898, when a white supremacist coup overthrew Wilmington's democratically elected biracial government.

county_specifics.note: Wilmington rebuilt its port economy but never regained its antebellum dominance. The city's Black community — artisans, stevedores, business owners, professionals — was the largest and most politically organized in NC. Cotton compresses, lumber mills, and railroad shops employed both races. The 1898 coup destroyed this community.

The old rice plantations were abandoned or converted to truck farming and lumber extraction. Smithville (renamed Southport in 1887) developed as a fishing and pilot station. The county remained rural and sparsely populated.

Lumber mills appeared along the Cape Fear River and its tributaries, cutting the remaining longleaf pine and cypress. Elizabethtown remained a small river town. Tenant farming on the old turpentine lands replaced the antebellum system.

Lumber became the dominant economy as sawmills moved through the remaining forests. Whiteville grew slowly as a lumber-shipping point. The Waccamaw Siouan community continued to exist largely outside the formal economy.
Created as a county in 1875 from New Hanover. Burgaw became the county seat. Turpentine operations persisted in diminished form. Lumber extraction expanded. The county was rural and isolated.

key_infrastructure.name: Wilmington & Weldon Railroad rebuilt
key_infrastructure.impact: Railroad reconstruction reconnected Wilmington to the national rail network. The Atlantic Coast Line Railroad (consolidated 1900) would make Wilmington its headquarters.

key_infrastructure.name: Sawmills throughout the pine belt
key_infrastructure.impact: Steam-powered sawmills replaced the colonial-era pit-saw method, enabling rapid extraction of the remaining longleaf pine and bald cypress forests. Lumber was shipped via river or rail to Wilmington for export.

key_infrastructure.name: Wilmington cotton compresses
key_infrastructure.impact: Cotton compresses prepared cotton bales for export, employing hundreds of Black workers. The compress district along the waterfront was a center of Black employment.

key_infrastructure.name: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad headquarters (Wilmington)
key_infrastructure.impact: The ACL consolidated several smaller lines and chose Wilmington as its headquarters, making the railroad the city's largest employer and ensuring Wilmington's continued importance as a transportation hub.

major_employers.name: Wilmington port operations and cotton compresses
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of stevedores, compress workers, draymen, and warehouse hands — predominantly Black.

major_employers.name: Sawmills and lumber operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Dozens of mills employing hundreds of workers total. Mill labor was mixed race.

major_employers.name: Sharecropping and tenant farming
major_employers.employment_scale: The dominant employment mode in the rural counties. Both Black and white families worked on shares.

major_employers.name: Turpentine operations (declining)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining as forests were exhausted. Turpentine peonage system trapped Black workers in debt bondage.

wage_benchmarks.source: Reaves, William M. Strength Through Struggle: The Chronological and Historical Record of the African American Community in Wilmington, NC, 1865-1950, ch. 3.
Outland, Tapping the Pines, ch. 6.
Ransom and Sutch, One Kind of Freedom, ch. 4.

wage_benchmarks.wage: $0.50-$0.75/day (when paid at all — peonage system often negated wages)
wage_benchmarks.source: Outland, Tapping the Pines, ch. 7.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: After the war, {ancestor_name} found work in a {county} County economy that was cutting down what was left of the longleaf pines — the same trees that had sustained {pronoun_possessive} grandparents' turpentine. The lumber went to Wilmington, the turpentine was nearly gone, and the land that remained was rented, not owned.
economic_eras.source: Reaves, William M. Strength Through Struggle: The Chronological and Historical Record of the African American Community in Wilmington, North Carolina, 1865-1950. Ed. Beverly Tetterton. Wilmington: New Hanover County Public Library, 1998. Outland, Tapping the Pines. Ransom, Roger L., and Richard Sutch. One Kind of Freedom. 2nd ed. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: White supremacist economic restructuring; expulsion of Black professionals and business owners; Atlantic Coast Line Railroad as dominant employer; lumber industry winding down; commercial agriculture expanding in interior counties
economic_eras.description: The Wilmington coup of November 10, 1898 — the only successful armed overthrow of an elected government in United States history — was an economic event as much as a political one. The white supremacist conspirators targeted not only Black officeholders but Black business owners, professionals, and skilled workers whose economic success threatened the racial caste system. Over 2,100 Black citizens were expelled from Wilmington in the days following the coup. Black-owned businesses were destroyed or seized. The Daily Record, the nation's only Black-owned daily newspaper, was burned to the ground. The economic consequences radiated outward: Black professionals and entrepreneurs throughout the Lower Cape Fear received an unmistakable message that success could be violently punished. In the years following, Wilmington's Black population declined as families left for northern cities — an early wave of what would become the Great Migration. The white business elite consolidated control over the port, the railroads, and the commercial infrastructure. The Atlantic Coast Line Railroad, headquartered in Wilmington after 1900, became the city's dominant employer and the region's largest corporate presence.

county_specifics.note: Wilmington was the epicenter. Black citizens murdered, expelled, businesses destroyed. The biracial government was replaced by an all-white regime. The city's Black middle class — the largest in NC — was decapitated. Economic power consolidated in white hands.
Rural and removed from direct violence but affected by the regional climate of terror. Black families in Brunswick County who had achieved landowner or tenant status faced intensified pressure.
The 1898 white supremacy campaign was statewide, and Bladen County's political dynamics shifted. Black voter registration collapsed after the 1900 constitutional amendment.
Similarly affected by the statewide disfranchisement campaign. The Waccamaw Siouan community, classified ambiguously in racial records, faced particular vulnerability.
Rural and isolated, but the economic ripple effects of the coup reached even here — Black workers who had maintained a degree of economic autonomy found that autonomy under threat.

key_infrastructure.name: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad headquarters (Wilmington)
key_infrastructure.impact: The ACL became Wilmington's largest employer, with repair shops, offices, and rail yards. Railroad employment was rigidly segregated — Black workers held the hardest, lowest-paid positions (section hands, porters, laborers) while white workers held skilled and supervisory roles.

key_infrastructure.name: Wilmington port modernization
key_infrastructure.impact: Port improvements kept Wilmington competitive as a shipping point for lumber, cotton, and naval stores, but the city's port never recovered its antebellum dominance as Charleston and Norfolk captured market share.

key_infrastructure.name: Lumber railroads (interior counties)
key_infrastructure.impact: Short-line railroads penetrated the remaining forests of Bladen, Columbus, and Pender counties, enabling rapid timber extraction. These lines were temporary — once the timber was cut, the rails were pulled up and the communities they served withered.

major_employers.name: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad
major_employers.employment_scale: Wilmington's largest employer — several thousand workers in offices, repair shops, and operating crews.

major_employers.name: Lumber mills (diminishing)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining as forests were cut out. Sawmills moved or closed as timber was exhausted.

major_employers.name: Wilmington port operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred workers. Port employment was one of the few areas where Black workers retained skilled positions (pilots, caulkers) despite the post-coup racial restructuring.

wage_benchmarks.source: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad records; NC Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Atlantic Coast Line Railroad records.
Outland, Tapping the Pines, ch. 8.
Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 4.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: After November 10, 1898, the economy of the Lower Cape Fear was remade by violence. The Black business owners, the professionals, the skilled workers who had built Wilmington's prosperity alongside their white neighbors were expelled or silenced — and {ancestor_name} lived in the aftermath, where the rules had been rewritten at gunpoint.
economic_eras.source: Umfleet, LeRae. A Day of Blood: The 1898 Wilmington Race Riot. Raleigh: NC Office of Archives and History, 2009. Prather, H. Leon, Sr. We Have Taken a City: The Wilmington Racial Massacre and Coup of 1898. Cranbury, NJ: Associated University Presses, 1984. Reaves, Strength Through Struggle.

economic_eras.era_name: Interwar Era — Agriculture, Fishing, and Early Tourism
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Tobacco and cotton agriculture in interior counties; commercial fishing along the coast; early tourism at Wrightsville Beach and Carolina Beach; railroad employment declining; Great Depression devastation
economic_eras.description: The interwar Lower Cape Fear was an economy in slow transition. The lumber boom was over — the great longleaf pine forests of Bladen, Columbus, and Pender counties had been cut, leaving cutover land that farmers converted to tobacco and cotton cultivation under the crop-lien system. Wilmington's port and railroad economy sustained the city but did not grow. The coast began attracting seasonal tourists to Wrightsville Beach and Carolina Beach, introducing a service economy that would eventually transform the region. Commercial fishing — menhaden, shrimp, oysters — employed hundreds of men along the Brunswick and New Hanover coastline. The Great Depression hit the agricultural interior hard; tobacco prices collapsed from 18 cents per pound in 1929 to 8 cents by 1931. The Agricultural Adjustment Act (1933) introduced tobacco and cotton allotments that stabilized prices but accelerated the displacement of tenant families.

county_specifics.note: Wilmington's economy was sustained by the ACL Railroad, the port, and a modest manufacturing sector. Wrightsville Beach and Carolina Beach attracted increasing tourist traffic. The city's population grew slowly — from 25,000 in 1920 to 33,000 in 1940.
Fishing, truck farming, and modest tourism at Southport and Long Beach. The county remained one of the least populated and poorest in NC. The old rice lands were swamp and scrub.

Tobacco and cotton replaced turpentine and lumber as the primary cash crops on the cutover land. Elizabethtown remained a small agricultural service town. The county was deeply rural and economically depressed.

Similar transition to tobacco and cotton agriculture. The lumber industry was largely exhausted. Tabor City and Whiteville served as small market towns. The Waccamaw Siouan community maintained a separate school system.
Tobacco and truck farming on cutover pine land. Burgaw served as a small county seat. Pender was among the most isolated counties in the region.

key_infrastructure.name: Agricultural Adjustment Act — tobacco and cotton allotments
key_infrastructure.impact: Federal allotments stabilized commodity prices but reduced acreage and accelerated tenant displacement in the interior counties. Allotments were assigned to landowners, giving landlords leverage over tenants.

key_infrastructure.name: Wrightsville Beach and Carolina Beach development
key_infrastructure.impact: Beach resort development brought seasonal tourism to New Hanover County — hotels, pavilions, and the trolley line to Wrightsville Beach. This was the embryo of the service economy that would eventually dominate the coast.

key_infrastructure.name: Highway improvements (US 17, US 74/76)
key_infrastructure.impact: Paved highways connected Wilmington to the interior counties and to out-of-state markets. US 17 along the coast and US 74/76 through Columbus County reduced the isolation of the rural interior.

major_employers.name: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad
major_employers.employment_scale: Still Wilmington's largest single employer, though the workforce was declining as rail traffic stagnated.

major_employers.name: Tobacco and cotton farming
major_employers.employment_scale: The dominant employment in the interior counties, organized around sharecropping and tenant farming.

major_employers.name: Commercial fishing fleet
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of fishermen and processing workers. Menhaden factories at Southport and along the coast employed seasonal labor.

major_employers.name: Beach resort and tourism
major_employers.employment_scale: Seasonal — hundreds of workers in hotels, restaurants, and beach services during summer months.

wage_benchmarks.source: Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land, ch. 4.
Taylor, Joseph E. III. Making Salmon: An Environmental History. Seattle: University of Washington Press, 1999 (comparative fisheries wages).
Atlantic Coast Line Railroad payroll records.
Wrightsville Beach historical records.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, the great longleaf forests that had built the Lower Cape Fear economy were stumps — {ancestor_name} farmed tobacco on cutover land in {county} County, working soil that two generations earlier had been shaded by pines a hundred feet tall.
economic_eras.source: Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985. Outland, Tapping the Pines.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Wilmington shipyard (North Carolina Shipbuilding Company) and Camp Davis as wartime transformers; postwar military installations; early beach development; tobacco allotments sustaining interior agriculture
economic_eras.description: World War II broke the Lower Cape Fear's decades-long economic stagnation. The North Carolina Shipbuilding Company, built on the Wilmington waterfront in 1941, employed over 20,000 workers at its peak — making it the largest employer in North Carolina history to that date. The shipyard built 243 Liberty ships and other vessels, drawing workers from across eastern NC and the rural South. For the first time, thousands of Black and white workers earned industrial wages — $0.88/hour starting pay, which dwarfed agricultural earnings. Camp Davis in Pender County, an anti-aircraft training facility, brought 20,000 military personnel to an area that had been pine forest and farmland. The economic injection was transformative and immediate: Wilmington's population surged, housing was desperately scarce, and the city experienced the social upheaval that came with rapid industrialization. After the war, the shipyard closed and Camp Davis was deactivated, but the economic transformation was permanent. Workers who had earned industrial wages would not return to sharecropping. The beach communities (Wrightsville Beach, Carolina Beach, Kure Beach) began developing as year-round communities rather than seasonal resorts. By 1960, the Lower Cape Fear was a fundamentally different economy than it had been in 1940.

county_specifics.note: The North Carolina Shipbuilding Company dominated the wartime economy. Wilmington's population surged from 33,000 to over 50,000 during the war. Housing shortages were severe. The waterfront was transformed from commercial docks to industrial shipyard. After the war, the economy contracted before beginning a slow pivot toward services and tourism.

Camp Davis (1941-1944), built near Holly Ridge, was an anti-aircraft artillery training center with 20,000 military personnel. The camp transformed the local landscape — roads were paved, utilities installed, and the town of Holly Ridge grew from nothing to several thousand people. When Camp Davis closed in 1944, the infrastructure remained and some families stayed.

Military activity at Fort Caswell (converted to a Baptist assembly ground after the war) and coastal defense installations brought temporary military presence. Sunny Point Army Terminal, built in the 1950s, became a permanent military munitions facility and a major employer. Beach development at Long Beach (now Oak Island) began.

The agricultural economy continued under tobacco allotments, but the departure of working-age people to Wilmington's shipyard and Camp Davis drained the county's labor force. Some returned after the war; many did not. Elizabethtown gained a small manufacturing sector.

Similar to Bladen — agricultural economy with increasing out-migration. Tobacco and some livestock farming. Tabor City's border position drew South Carolina commerce. The Waccamaw Siouan community maintained separate institutions but gained increased recognition.

key_infrastructure.name: North Carolina Shipbuilding Company (Wilmington)
key_infrastructure.impact: Built 243 vessels — Liberty ships, tankers, and other craft — employing over 20,000 workers at peak. The largest industrial facility in NC history to that date. Drew workers from across the rural South. Wages of $0.88/hour (starting) transformed the economic expectations of an entire generation.

key_infrastructure.name: Camp Davis (Pender County)
key_infrastructure.impact: Anti-aircraft artillery training center near Holly Ridge. Brought 20,000 military personnel to rural Pender County. Construction alone employed thousands. Infrastructure (roads, water, power) permanently improved the area.

key_infrastructure.name: Sunny Point Army Terminal (Brunswick County)
key_infrastructure.impact: Military ammunition loading and shipping facility on the Cape Fear River. Became a permanent military installation and major employer in Brunswick County — the county's largest non-government employer.

key_infrastructure.name: Beach community development
key_infrastructure.impact: Postwar prosperity and automobile access drove residential and commercial development at Wrightsville Beach, Carolina Beach, and emerging Brunswick County beach communities. Tourism and real estate began replacing heavy industry as economic drivers.

major_employers.name: North Carolina Shipbuilding Company
major_employers.employment_scale: Over 20,000 workers at peak — the largest single employer in NC history. Workforce included Black and white workers, men and women. Segregated facilities but integrated payroll.

major_employers.name: Camp Davis
major_employers.employment_scale: 20,000 military personnel plus thousands of civilian construction and service workers.

major_employers.name: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad
major_employers.employment_scale: Still a major employer but declining as highway freight competed with rail.

major_employers.name: Sunny Point Army Terminal
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred military and civilian workers — Brunswick County's largest employer.

major_employers.name: Tobacco farming (allotment-regulated)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining as mechanization and allotment consolidation reduced labor needs.

wage_benchmarks.source: Mobley, Joe A. Ship Ashore! The U.S. Lifesavers of Coastal North Carolina. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1994; Wilmington Morning Star wartime reporting.
U.S. Army payroll records.
Daniel, Breaking the Land, ch. 5.
NC Division of Marine Fisheries historical records.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When the shipyard opened on the Wilmington waterfront in 1941, {ancestor_name} could earn more in a week than a month of sharecropping in {county} County had ever paid. The war did what no amount of cotton or tobacco ever could — it put cash money in the hands of people who had never held it before.
economic_eras.source: Fonvielle, Chris E., Jr. Wilmington, North Carolina, During World War II. Unpublished manuscript, UNCW Special Collections. Daniel, Breaking the Land. Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.

economic_turning_points.event: Completion of the Wilmington & Weldon Railroad
economic_turning_points.impact: The longest railroad in the world connected Wilmington's port to the Virginia border, channeling the commerce of eastern NC through the city. Wilmington became both a port and a railroad hub — the only city in NC with both. The railroad made Wilmington the state's largest city and ensured that the Lower Cape Fear economy was connected to national markets in a way no other NC region could match.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The railroad that reached Wilmington in {year} connected {ancestor_name}'s world to everywhere else — turpentine, lumber, cotton, and people could now move from the Lower Cape Fear to Virginia, to New York, to anywhere the rails went.
economic_turning_points.source: Watson, Alan D. Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2003.

economic_turning_points.event: Fall of Fort Fisher and occupation of Wilmington
economic_turning_points.impact: The Union capture of Fort Fisher on January 15, 1865, closed the Confederacy's last port. Wilmington was occupied on February 22, 1865. The emancipation of the region's enslaved majority — roughly half the population — eliminated the labor system on which rice, turpentine, and port operations had all depended. The economic disruption was total.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When Fort Fisher fell in January 1865, the Cape Fear's economy fell with it — the port closed, the blockade runners vanished, and the people who had built the region's wealth were free to leave the plantations that had held them.
economic_turning_points.source: Fonvielle, Chris E., Jr. The Wilmington Campaign. Campbell, CA: Savas Publishing, 1997.

economic_turning_points.impact: The armed overthrow of Wilmington's elected government was the most destructive single event in the Lower Cape Fear's economic history after the Civil War. The expulsion of over 2,100 Black citizens — including business owners, skilled workers, and professionals — removed a generation of Black economic achievement. The destruction of the Daily Record eliminated the nation's only Black-owned daily newspaper. The coup's economic legacy was a city where Black enterprise was suppressed and Black workers were confined to the lowest-paid, most dangerous positions for generations afterward.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: November 10, 1898, was not a riot — it was the deliberate destruction of Black economic life in Wilmington. The businesses that were burned, the professionals who were expelled, the workers who were terrorized — the coup took from {ancestor_name}'s community not just political power but the economic foundation that community had built from nothing in thirty-three years.
economic_turning_points.source: Umfleet, LeRae. A Day of Blood: The 1898 Wilmington Race Riot. Raleigh: NC Office of Archives and History, 2009.

economic_turning_points.impact: The Wilmington shipyard was the most transformative economic event in the Lower Cape Fear since the arrival of the railroad. Over 20,000 workers earned industrial wages for the first time. The shipyard drew labor from across eastern NC, depopulating rural farming counties. The economic expectations of an entire generation were permanently altered — workers who had earned $0.88/hour would not return to sharecropping at $0.50/day.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The shipyard changed the math for {ancestor_name} and every family in the Lower Cape Fear — for the first time, a week's wages exceeded a month of farming, and the calculation of whether to stay on the land or leave it was settled.
economic_turning_points.source: Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.

narrative_hooks: In {county} County in the {decade}s, the economy ran through Wilmington's port — the only deep-water harbor in North Carolina, and the funnel through which every barrel of turpentine, every board foot of lumber, and every bale of cotton had to pass.

When {ancestor_name} looked for work in the Lower Cape Fear around {year}, the options depended on what the land and the river could still give — turpentine if the pines were standing, lumber if they were being cut, farming if the stumps had been pulled.
The blockade runners made fortunes in gold while {ancestor_name}'s family in {county} County ate what the garden could produce. The war's economy was as unequal as the peace that followed it.

After November 10, 1898, the economy of Wilmington was rewritten — the Black businesses destroyed, the professionals expelled, the skilled workers terrorized into accepting whatever the new order offered.

When the shipyard opened on the Wilmington waterfront in 1941, it drew people from every county in the Lower Cape Fear — {ancestor_name} among them, trading a life measured in crop years for one measured in weekly paychecks.

By the {decade}s, the rice fields of Brunswick County were marsh again, the turpentine forests of Bladen were stumps, and the economy that {ancestor_name} inherited had worn through two natural resources and was working on a third.
source_references: Lee, Lawrence. The Lower Cape Fear in Colonial Days. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1965.
Watson, Alan D. Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2003.
Carney, Judith A. Black Rice: The African Origins of Rice Cultivation in the Americas. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2001.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 2004.
Wise, Stephen R. Lifeline of the Confederacy: Blockade Running During the Civil War. Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1988.
Fonvielle, Chris E., Jr. The Wilmington Campaign: Last Rays of Departing Hope. Campbell, CA: Savas Publishing, 1997.
Gragg, Rod. Confederate Goliath: The Battle of Fort Fisher. New York: HarperCollins, 1991.
Umfleet, LeRae. A Day of Blood: The 1898 Wilmington Race Riot. Raleigh: NC Office of Archives and History, 2009.
Prather, H. Leon, Sr. We Have Taken a City: The Wilmington Racial Massacre and Coup of 1898. Cranbury, NJ: Associated University Presses, 1984.

Reaves, William M. Strength Through Struggle: The Chronological and Historical Record of the African American Community in Wilmington, NC, 1865-1950. Ed. Beverly Tetterton. Wilmington: New Hanover County Public Library, 1998.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
Ransom, Roger L., and Richard Sutch. One Kind of Freedom: The Economic Consequences of Emancipation. 2nd ed. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001.
Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.
